[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who already holds isAuthorized[victim][attacker]==true on Midnight sign a fresh Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=attacker2, isAuthorized=true, nonce=currentNonce, deadline=far} and submit it to EcrecoverAuthorizer, passing the signer==authorization.authorizer||isAuthorized(authorizer,signer) check, thereby granting attacker2 full Midnight control over victim, who then calls withdraw() to redeem victim's credit for more loan tokens than victim is owed? Preconditions: victim has credit in a market at or after maturity; attacker is already authorized on Midnight for victim. Call sequence: (1) attacker signs Authorization struct with block.chainid domain, (2) EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized called, (3) attacker2 calls Midnight.withdraw(market, victim.credit, victim, attacker2). Invariant tested: no unauthorized party may redeem credit on behalf of another user. Scoped impact: credit/debt units redeemed for more assets than economically owed. Proof idea: unit test with mock Midnight stub; assert attacker2 receives loan tokens without victim consent.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER exploit the transitive authorization chain—where any address authorized on Midnight for the authorizer may sign an Authorization struct—to cascade authorization to an arbitrary third address, then call Midnight.withdraw() post-maturity to drain the victim's credit balance? Preconditions: victim authorized attacker (e.g., via a smart-contract operator) on Midnight; market is past maturity; victim has nonzero credit. Call sequence: (1) attacker signs Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=attacker2, isAuthorized=true}, (2) EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized succeeds because isAuthorized[victim][attacker]==true, (3) attacker2 calls withdraw(market, credit, victim, attacker2). Invariant tested: authorization delegation must not allow unbounded transitive extension that enables asset extraction. Scoped impact: lender credit redeemed for more assets than owed. Proof idea: stateful fuzz test varying authorization depth and assert victim token balance never decreases without victim action.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER submit a valid Authorization signature with isAuthorized=false to revoke a victim's own operator authorization, then immediately submit a second Authorization with isAuthorized=true for the attacker, using the incremented nonce, to seize control and call withdraw() to over-redeem the victim's credit at maturity? Preconditions: victim has an existing operator; victim has credit in a settled market; attacker knows victim's current nonce. Call sequence: (1) attacker submits Authorization{isAuthorized=false, authorized=victim_operator, nonce=N} signed by attacker (who is authorized), (2) attacker submits Authorization{isAuthorized=true, authorized=attacker, nonce=N+1}, (3) attacker calls withdraw(). Invariant tested: nonce sequence must not allow attacker to both revoke and re-authorize in a single atomic sequence. Scoped impact: unauthorized credit redemption. Proof idea: two-step PoC asserting nonce increments atomically and victim operator is revoked before attacker gains access.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER front-run a victim's own setIsAuthorized call (e.g., victim revoking an operator) by submitting a higher-gas transaction that uses the same nonce to authorize the attacker instead, causing the victim's revocation to revert with InvalidNonce, leaving the attacker authorized and able to call withdraw() to drain credit? Preconditions: victim broadcasts a revocation tx; attacker observes mempool; victim has credit at or near maturity. Call sequence: (1) attacker front-runs with Authorization{authorizer=victim, authorized=attacker, isAuthorized=true, nonce=N} signed by attacker (already authorized), (2) victim's revocation tx reverts, (3) attacker calls withdraw(). Invariant tested: nonce-based replay protection must not be front-runnable to block revocation. Scoped impact: credit drained via unauthorized withdrawal. Proof idea: fork test simulating mempool ordering; assert victim revocation always succeeds before attacker authorization.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER exploit the fact that EcrecoverAuthorizer uses block.chainid in its domain separator while Midnight uses INITIAL_CHAIN_ID for market IDs, so that after a hard fork a signature valid on the pre-fork chain is invalid on the post-fork chain but the same Midnight market state persists, allowing an attacker to replay a pre-fork authorization signature on the post-fork chain if block.chainid reverts to the original value, and then call withdraw() to over-redeem credit? Preconditions: hard fork changes then reverts block.chainid; victim has credit in a market whose ID was computed with INITIAL_CHAIN_ID; attacker has a pre-fork signed Authorization. Call sequence: (1) fork reverts chainid, (2) attacker submits pre-fork Authorization, (3) attacker calls withdraw(). Invariant tested: domain separator must be consistent with market ID derivation across chain ID changes. Scoped impact: credit redeemed without valid post-fork authorization. Proof idea: differential test comparing domain separator computation in EcrecoverAuthorizer vs IdLib.toId across chainid values.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER submit an Authorization with deadline == block.timestamp and have it accepted (require(block.timestamp <= authorization.deadline)), then immediately call withdraw() in the same block to drain credit, bypassing any off-chain deadline monitoring that assumes deadline < block.timestamp is required? Preconditions: victim has credit; attacker is authorized on Midnight for victim; deadline == block.timestamp. Call sequence: (1) EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized with deadline==block.timestamp, (2) attacker calls withdraw() in same block. Invariant tested: deadline boundary must be exclusive or inclusive consistently with off-chain expectations. Scoped impact: credit redeemed in same block as authorization, bypassing monitoring. Proof idea: unit test with vm.warp to exact deadline; assert setIsAuthorized succeeds and withdraw executes.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER craft a signature with a non-standard v value (e.g., v=0 or v=1 instead of 27/28) that ecrecover accepts and returns a nonzero address matching an authorized address, thereby passing the Unauthorized check and granting the attacker authorization to call withdraw() and over-redeem credit? Preconditions: ecrecover behavior with non-standard v; victim has credit at maturity. Call sequence: (1) attacker crafts (v,r,s) with v not in {27,28} but ecrecover returns authorized address, (2) EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized succeeds, (3) attacker calls withdraw(). Invariant tested: signature validation must reject non-canonical v values. Scoped impact: unauthorized credit redemption. Proof idea: fuzz (v,r,s) with v in {0,1,2,3,27,28} and assert only canonical signatures are accepted.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER exploit EIP-2098 compact signature malleability—where (v,r,s) and (v',r,s') produce the same recovered address—to replay an already-consumed authorization signature with a different (v,r,s) encoding, bypassing the nonce check if the nonce was not incremented for the malleated variant, and then call withdraw() to over-redeem credit? Preconditions: victim's nonce is at N after a legitimate authorization; attacker has the original (v,r,s); malleated variant recovers same signer. Call sequence: (1) attacker submits malleated Authorization with nonce=N+1 (next valid nonce), (2) EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized succeeds, (3) attacker calls withdraw(). Invariant tested: signature malleability must not allow replay of authorization intent. Scoped impact: unauthorized credit redemption. Proof idea: unit test with both canonical and malleated signatures; assert only one succeeds per nonce.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized + Midnight.withdraw] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who obtains authorization via EcrecoverAuthorizer call Midnight.withdraw() on behalf of a victim lender before _updatePosition is triggered, and if the victim's position has a stale lastLossFactor (i.e., lossFactor has increased since last interaction), does the attacker receive more loan tokens than the victim's post-slash credit entitles them to? Preconditions: bad debt was realized after victim's last interaction; victim's position.lastLossFactor < marketState.lossFactor; attacker is authorized. Call sequence: (1) bad debt realized, lossFactor increases, (2) attacker calls withdraw(market, victim.credit, victim, attacker) — withdraw calls _updatePosition first. Invariant tested: withdraw must apply current lossFactor before transferring assets. Scoped impact: verify _updatePosition in withdraw prevents over-redemption; if skipped in any path, credit/debt units redeem for more than owed. Proof idea: unit test asserting withdraw(units=victim.credit) transfers exactly updatePositionView(victim).newCredit tokens.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: Midnight.updatePositionView + withdraw] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER (authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer) call withdraw() with units == position.credit (stale value) when the actual post-slash credit from updatePositionView is lower, causing an underflow revert or, if the check is insufficient, transferring more assets than the post-slash credit? Preconditions: lossFactor increased since victim's last interaction; attacker authorized; victim has nonzero credit. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls withdraw(market, position[id][victim].credit, victim, attacker), (2) _updatePosition reduces credit to newCredit < units, (3) credit -= units underflows. Invariant tested: withdraw must revert or correctly limit units to post-slash credit. Scoped impact: if underflow is not caught, over-redemption occurs. Proof idea: fuzz lossFactor and credit values; assert withdraw(units > newCredit) always reverts.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: Midnight.updatePositionView] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER (authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer) exploit rounding in updatePositionView where postSlashCredit = credit.mulDivDown(...) rounds down but postSlashPendingFee = pendingFee - pendingFee.mulDivUp(credit - postSlashCredit, credit) rounds up the deduction, causing pendingFee to be understated, so the lender's effective credit (credit - pendingFee) is overstated, and withdraw() transfers more assets than economically owed? Preconditions: victim has credit with nonzero pendingFee; lossFactor has increased; attacker authorized. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls withdraw(market, newCredit, victim, attacker) after _updatePosition, (2) newCredit = postSlashCredit - fee where fee = postSlashPendingFee.mulDivDown(...). Invariant tested: sum of all withdrawable credits must not exceed marketState.withdrawable. Scoped impact: rounding leakage allows lender to redeem more than owed. Proof idea: invariant fuzz test asserting sum(position[id][u].credit for all u) <= marketState.totalUnits after every operation.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: Midnight.updatePositionView] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER (authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer) trigger a scenario where updatePositionView returns newCredit > 0 but the continuous fee accrual formula fee = postSlashPendingFee.mulDivDown(accrualEnd - lastAccrual, maturity - lastAccrual) produces fee=0 due to integer division truncation when (accrualEnd - lastAccrual) is very small, allowing the lender to withdraw credit without paying the accrued continuous fee, effectively redeeming more than economically owed? Preconditions: lastAccrual is very close to block.timestamp; maturity is far; pendingFee is small. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls withdraw() immediately after a prior interaction, (2) fee rounds to 0, (3) full credit is withdrawn. Invariant tested: continuous fee must be accrued proportionally; zero-fee withdrawal must not be achievable by timing. Scoped impact: lender redeems credit without paying continuous fee. Proof idea: fuzz (lastAccrual, block.timestamp, maturity, pendingFee) and assert fee > 0 whenever pendingFee > 0 and accrualEnd > lastAccrual.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: Midnight.withdraw + claimContinuousFee] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER (authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer) call withdraw() for a victim lender after claimContinuousFee has reduced marketState.withdrawable below the sum of all lenders' post-slash credits, causing the last lender to withdraw to receive fewer assets than their credit entitles them to (fund freeze), or conversely, can the first lender withdraw more than their share if withdrawable is not reduced proportionally? Preconditions: continuousFeeCredit > 0; feeClaimer claims full continuousFeeCredit; multiple lenders have credit. Call sequence: (1) feeClaimer calls claimContinuousFee(market, continuousFeeCredit, receiver), (2) attacker calls withdraw(market, victim.credit, victim, attacker). Invariant tested: marketState.withdrawable >= sum of all lenders' redeemable credit after fee claims. Scoped impact: over-redemption by early withdrawers or fund freeze for late withdrawers. Proof idea: invariant test with N lenders; assert sum of all withdraw amounts == initial repaid assets.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: Midnight.take + withdraw post-maturity] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER (authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer for a lender) take a sell offer post-maturity (offer.buy=true, taker=lender) to increase the lender's credit beyond what was repaid, then call withdraw() to redeem the inflated credit for more loan tokens than the market's withdrawable balance supports? Preconditions: market is post-maturity; withdrawable < totalUnits; attacker authorized for lender; a buy offer exists at price < 1. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls take(buyOffer, ..., units, lender, ...) increasing lender credit, (2) attacker calls withdraw(market, newCredit, lender, attacker). Invariant tested: totalUnits must not exceed withdrawable + outstanding debt after maturity. Scoped impact: credit redeemed for more assets than economically owed. Proof idea: state test asserting marketState.withdrawable >= sum(credit) for all lenders after any sequence of post-maturity takes and withdrawals.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: Midnight.repay + withdraw] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER (authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer for a borrower) call repay() with a callback that reverts after Midnight has already updated position[id][borrower].debt -= units and marketState.withdrawable += units, causing the repay to revert and roll back, but if the callback is a reentrancy vector that calls withdraw() before the revert, can the attacker extract assets from withdrawable without actually repaying debt? Preconditions: attacker authorized for borrower; borrower has debt; withdrawable > 0; attacker controls callback. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls repay(market, units, borrower, attackerCallback, data), (2) Midnight updates debt and withdrawable, (3) callback reenters withdraw() before repay's transferFrom, (4) callback returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS, (5) transferFrom pulls tokens. Invariant tested: withdrawable must not be accessible before repay's token transfer completes. Scoped impact: credit redeemed for more assets than repaid. Proof idea: reentrancy PoC asserting withdraw inside repay callback cannot extract assets beyond pre-repay withdrawable.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: Midnight.liquidate + withdraw] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER (authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer for a lender) call liquidate() with badDebt > 0 to realize bad debt and increase lossFactor, then immediately call withdraw() for the lender before other lenders' positions are updated, allowing the first lender to withdraw at the pre-slash credit value while subsequent lenders are slashed more? Preconditions: market has bad debt; multiple lenders; attacker authorized for one lender. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls liquidate(market, ..., borrower, ...) realizing badDebt, (2) attacker calls withdraw(market, lender.credit, lender, attacker) before other lenders interact. Invariant tested: bad debt must be socialized proportionally; no lender may withdraw at pre-slash credit after lossFactor increases. Scoped impact: lender redeems more than post-slash entitlement. Proof idea: invariant test asserting sum of all post-slash credits equals totalUnits after bad debt realization.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: Midnight.liquidate bad debt + updatePositionView] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER (authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer) trigger double bad debt realization by calling liquidate() twice on the same borrower with seizedAssets=0 and repaidUnits=0, causing lossFactor to be updated twice for the same badDebt, over-slashing lenders' credit and then allowing the attacker to withdraw the 'freed' withdrawable assets? Preconditions: borrower has debt with no collateral (or all collateral seized); badDebt > 0; attacker authorized. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls liquidate(market, 0, 0, 0, borrower, false, ...) — badDebt realized, debt reduced, (2) attacker calls liquidate again — borrower.debt is now 0, NotBorrower() reverts. Invariant tested: bad debt must be realized exactly once per debt unit. Scoped impact: verify NotBorrower() prevents double realization; if bypassable, lenders are over-slashed and withdrawable is incorrect. Proof idea: unit test asserting second liquidate with debt=0 reverts with NotBorrower.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: Midnight.updatePositionView lossFactor edge case] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER (authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer) exploit the case where position.lastLossFactor == type(uint128).max (market loss factor maxed out) causing updatePositionView to return postSlashCredit=0, and then call withdraw(market, 0, victim, attacker) as a no-op, but if the attacker can manipulate the market to reach lossFactor == type(uint128).max - 1 and then trigger one more bad debt realization to max it out, can they drain withdrawable assets that belong to other lenders? Preconditions: market near lossFactor max; attacker authorized; other lenders have credit. Call sequence: (1) attacker triggers bad debt to push lossFactor to type(uint128).max, (2) all lenders' credit becomes 0 on next updatePosition, (3) attacker calls withdraw(market, 0, ...) — no-op, (4) attacker checks if withdrawable > 0 and calls claimContinuousFee or other path. Invariant tested: when lossFactor is maxed, all credit must be zero and withdrawable must equal only repaid-but-unclaimed assets. Scoped impact: residual withdrawable assets extracted without valid credit claim. Proof idea: state test asserting withdrawable == 0 when lossFactor == type(uint128).max and all debt is zero.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: Midnight.take settlement fee + withdraw] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER (authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer for a lender) take a sell offer at maturity boundary (timeToMaturity=0) where settlementFee uses the cbp0 breakpoint, and if cbp0 is set to 0 (zero settlement fee), the sellerPrice == buyerPrice == offerPrice, causing the fee spread to be zero, and then call withdraw() to redeem credit at face value, effectively bypassing the settlement fee that should reduce the lender's net return? Preconditions: market settlementFeeCbp0 == 0; block.timestamp == market.maturity; attacker authorized for lender. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls take(offer, ...) at maturity with zero fee, (2) lender receives full offerPrice * units assets, (3) attacker calls withdraw(). Invariant tested: settlement fee must be charged at all TTM breakpoints including TTM=0. Scoped impact: lender redeems more than economically owed due to zero fee. Proof idea: fuzz settlementFeeCbp0 in {0, 1, max} and assert claimableSettlementFee increases by nonzero amount for nonzero fee.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: Midnight.take post-maturity + CannotIncreaseDebtPostMaturity] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER (authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer for a seller) take a sell offer at exactly block.timestamp == market.maturity (the boundary condition in require(block.timestamp <= offer.market.maturity || sellerDebtIncrease == 0)) where the condition allows debt increase because block.timestamp == maturity satisfies <=, and then call withdraw() to redeem credit funded by the new debt, over-redeeming assets? Preconditions: block.timestamp == market.maturity; sell offer exists; seller has no existing credit to net against; attacker authorized. Call sequence: (1) vm.warp(market.maturity), (2) attacker calls take(sellOffer, ..., units, seller, ...) — sellerDebtIncrease > 0 is allowed because block.timestamp <= maturity, (3) buyer's credit increases, (4) buyer calls withdraw(). Invariant tested: debt must not increase at or after maturity. Scoped impact: new debt created at maturity funds credit that is immediately withdrawn. Proof idea: unit test at block.timestamp == maturity asserting sellerDebtIncrease == 0 is enforced.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: Midnight.take + multicall + withdraw] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER (authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer) use Midnight.multicall() to atomically: (1) take a sell offer increasing a lender's credit, (2) call withdraw() to redeem that credit, all within a single transaction, bypassing any off-chain monitoring that expects credit and withdrawal to be separate blocks, and if the market's withdrawable is insufficient, does the multicall revert atomically or leave partial state? Preconditions: market has withdrawable > 0; attacker authorized for lender; sell offer exists. Call sequence: multicall([take(sellOffer,...), withdraw(market, units, lender, attacker)]). Invariant tested: multicall must not allow credit creation and immediate redemption to exceed withdrawable. Scoped impact: credit redeemed for more assets than withdrawable supports. Proof idea: multicall PoC asserting withdrawable >= units before and after the combined operation.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized + Midnight.setIsAuthorized] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER who is authorized on Midnight for victim call EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized to authorize themselves with isAuthorized=true, then call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker, true, victim) directly (bypassing EcrecoverAuthorizer), and then call withdraw() — does the direct Midnight.setIsAuthorized path allow authorization without nonce/deadline/signature checks, enabling permanent authorization that survives EcrecoverAuthorizer nonce invalidation? Preconditions: attacker is authorized on Midnight for victim; victim has credit. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls Midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker2, true, victim) directly, (2) attacker2 calls withdraw(). Invariant tested: Midnight's direct setIsAuthorized must require the caller to be the onBehalf address or already authorized. Scoped impact: authorization granted without signature, enabling credit drain. Proof idea: unit test asserting Midnight.setIsAuthorized(attacker2, true, victim) by attacker succeeds when isAuthorized[victim][attacker]==true.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: EcrecoverAuthorizer.setIsAuthorized nonce] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER observe that EcrecoverAuthorizer.nonce[authorizer] is a public mapping and pre-compute future nonces, then sign multiple Authorization structs with nonces N, N+1, N+2 in advance, and submit them in sequence to grant/revoke authorization repeatedly, ultimately leaving the attacker authorized and calling withdraw() to drain credit? Preconditions: attacker is authorized on Midnight for victim; victim has credit; attacker pre-signs N authorizations. Call sequence: (1) attacker submits Authorization{nonce=N, isAuthorized=true, authorized=attacker}, (2) attacker submits Authorization{nonce=N+1, isAuthorized=false, authorized=victim_operator}, (3) attacker calls withdraw(). Invariant tested: pre-signed authorization sequences must not allow attacker to manipulate authorization state in ways victim did not intend. Scoped impact: credit drained via pre-planned authorization manipulation. Proof idea: multi-step test with pre-signed authorization sequence; assert victim can always revoke by submitting their own nonce first.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: Midnight.withdraw + withdrawable accounting] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER (authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer) call withdraw() for a lender with units > marketState.withdrawable, causing an underflow revert in _marketState.withdrawable -= units, or if withdrawable is exactly equal to units, drain the entire withdrawable pool leaving other lenders unable to withdraw? Preconditions: multiple lenders have credit; withdrawable < sum of all credits (due to outstanding debt); attacker authorized for one lender. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls withdraw(market, lender.credit, lender, attacker) where lender.credit > withdrawable, (2) underflow revert. Invariant tested: withdraw must revert when units > withdrawable; no lender may withdraw more than their proportional share of withdrawable. Scoped impact: fund freeze for other lenders or over-redemption if underflow check is absent. Proof idea: invariant fuzz test asserting withdrawable >= 0 after any sequence of withdrawals.

[File: 'src/periphery/interfaces/IEcrecoverAuthorizer.sol'] [Function: Midnight.take + sellerAssets rounding + withdraw] Can an unprivileged ATTACKER (authorized via EcrecoverAuthorizer for a buyer) take a sell offer where buyerAssets = units.mulDivDown(buyerPrice, WAD) and sellerAssets = units.mulDivUp(sellerPrice, WAD), and the rounding causes buyerAssets - sellerAssets < actual settlement fee, so claimableSettlementFee is underfunded, and then call withdraw() to redeem credit that was funded by the underfunded fee, effectively redeeming more than the protocol collected? Preconditions: offer at tick near settlement fee boundary; units chosen to maximize rounding gap; attacker authorized. Call sequence: (1) attacker calls take(offer, ..., units, buyer, ...), (2) claimableSettlementFee += buyerAssets - sellerAssets (may be 0 due to rounding), (3) buyer calls withdraw(). Invariant tested: claimableSettlementFee must equal sum of (buyerAssets - sellerAssets) across all takes. Scoped impact: settlement fee undercollected; lenders redeem more than protocol can support. Proof idea: fuzz units and tick; assert claimableSettlementFee[loanToken] >= expected fee after each take.
